epigraph.text:
Give, and it will be given to you. A good measure, 
pressed down, shaken together and running over, will be poured into 
your lap.

content.text:
The distributive property is one of the most powerful tools in algebra. 
It allows us to "distribute" multiplication over addition and 
subtraction, connecting the operations in a beautiful way.

Consider this situation: you buy 3 bags of fruit, and each bag contains 
2 apples and 4 oranges. How many pieces of fruit do you have in total?
You could calculate this two ways:

items: **Method 1:** Each bag has $2 + 4 = 6$ pieces, so $3 \times 6 = 18$ pieces

**Method 2:** You have $3 \times 2 = 6$ apples and $3 \times 4 = 12$ oranges, 
 so $6 + 12 = 18$ pieces

content.text: Both methods give the same answer. This is the distributive property in action!

content.title: Distributive Property
content.content:
The **distributive property** states that 
multiplying a number by a sum is the same as multiplying by each addend 
and then adding:
\[
a(b + c) = ab + ac \quad \text{and} \quad a(b - c) = ab - ac
\]
The multiplication "distributes" over the addition or subtraction.

Just as God's blessings are distributed to all who receive them, the 
distributive property shows how multiplication spreads its effect to 
each term within the parentheses.

content.text: Let's practice with numerical examples first:

The real power of the distributive property appears when working with 
variables:

content.content:
When distributing a negative number, remember that the negative sign 
affects each product:
\[
-3(x + 5) = -3x - 15 \quad \text{(not $-3x + 15$)}
\]

content.title: Distributing negatives
content.solution:
1. $-(x + 3) = -1(x + 3) = -x - 3$

2. $-4(2y - 5) = -4 \cdot 2y - (-4) \cdot 5 = -8y - (-20) = -8y + 20$

3. $-1(a - b + c) = -a + b - c$
 
 *Note: A negative sign in front of parentheses changes all signs inside.*

content.text: Often you will need to distribute first, then combine like terms:

content.title: Simplifying with Distribution
content.content:
1. Apply the distributive property to remove parentheses

2. Identify like terms

3. Combine like terms

content.title: Scripture
content.content:
In Matthew 25:14-30, the Parable of the Talents shows a master 
distributing talents to his servants. Each servant receives differently, 
but they all multiply what they receive. Similarly, the distributive 
property multiplies each term equally, but then the results are gathered 
together like the final accounting.

marginNotes.text:
For whoever has will be given more, and 
they will have an abundance.

A generous person will prosper; whoever 
refreshes others will be refreshed.

marginNotes.author: Carl Friedrich Gauss
marginNotes.text:
Mathematics is the queen of the 
sciences and arithmetic the queen of mathematics.

marginNotes.author: Blaise Pascal
marginNotes.text: The more I see of men, the more I admire dogs.
